threads.note: Romero named this adaptation of Matheson's I Am Legend as the blueprint he worked from. Watch the besiegers at Price's door, then watch the farmhouse: the same shuffle, four years and one revolution apart.

Two industrial-film crews from the middle of the country, borrowing the company gear to make a horror picture. The pallid dead of Saltair walk again outside a Pennsylvania farmhouse — regional filmmaking becoming modern horror.
The word 'zombie' changes meaning between these two films: from the dead as owned labour to the dead as pure appetite. The whole politics of the genre is in that swap.
Two unauthorized routes out of Bram Stoker: Murnau rewrote Dracula and was sued into near-oblivion; a decade later Lugosi, fresh from playing the licensed Count, walked into the mill of the undead.
A somnambulist in a painted world; an organist one layer out of register with hers. Expressionism's central idea — the world bent to the shape of a mind — crosses forty years and an ocean intact.
Fritz Lang, twice: the city as a machine that consumes its workers, then the city as a set of small rooms that do the same job more quietly. The architecture shrank; the trap didn't.
The crowd as protagonist, staged two ways in two years: Eisenstein cuts the masses into a weapon; Lang choreographs them into a machine. Every stadium crowd shot since is quoting one or the other.
Two men narrating their own autopsies. Noir's boldest structural idea — the voice that already knows how this ends — done first on poverty row, then with a studio's polish.
The fatal ride, from both sides: Ulmer's drifter accepts one, Lupino's killer is one. Between the two films, the American road stops being freedom.

A quiet American town hosts a monster wearing ordinary clothes — first the war criminal grading papers, then the assassin at the window. Postwar noir kept asking the same question: what if he lives here?
The threat is upstairs. Hitchcock built suspense out of a household's suspicion; Suddenly locks the household in with the answer. Same architecture, opposite disclosure.
Ben Hecht's newsroom, twice over: the press that will hang a man for a headline and the press that will canonize a healthy woman for circulation. Screwball is the sugar; the indictment is the pill.
Carole Lombard's two registers, two years apart — the great comedienne in Technicolor farce, then playing a marriage almost entirely straight. The timing is identical; only the stakes change.
Class comedy at two speeds: Godfrey walks up from the dump into Fifth Avenue at a stroll; Hildy talks her way around the whole social order at 240 words a minute.

The two poles of Cary Grant, twenty-three years apart: the fast-talking operator who won't let her leave, and the unknowable charmer she probably shouldn't trust. Both films run on the same current — charm as suspense.
Keaton walks into the screen; Astaire walks up the wall. Two impossible spaces, both built with a camera trick played perfectly straight — the special effect hidden inside a straight face.
The machine as co-star, 1926 and 1927: Keaton loves his locomotive like a person; Lang fears his Machine like a god. Between those two feelings sits the whole century's argument with technology.

Two graduates of the Corman factory: a 24-year-old Coppola given a castle and a schedule, and a two-day comedy that hands Jack Nicholson his first unforgettable scene. The B-movie as film school, tuition paid in speed.
Horror as showmanship economics: Castle wired the seats, Corman beat the clock. Both films understand the audience is part of the machine — the scream is the product.

A love nearly destroyed by one decision, then rebuilt in public spaces — the tram and the city for Murnau, the liner and the Empire State for McCarey. The near-loss is what makes the tenderness legible.
